task: R6-B — New York PHL + SHIELD (alternate primary via NY AG)
disclaimer: Operational compliance research — not legal advice. NY may impose privacy standards more stringent than HIPAA; SHIELD applies to private information beyond HIPAA PHI.
policy: Public .gov sources only; nysenate statute text PARKED; AG official summary used as secondary for SHIELD requirements.

regulator.agency: New York State Department of Health

cited_facts.text: NY Department of Health maintains HIPAA regulation resources including preemption charts, access flow chart, and notices of privacy practices for DOH covered programs.
HIPAA did not supersede state laws imposing more stringent privacy standards; NY DOH states it will enforce such state laws within its purview.
PHL §17 prevails over HIPAA where a provider must release medical records to another designated physician or hospital upon written patient request.
PHL §18 governs patient information held by health care practitioners; NY DOH preemption charts map scope differences vs. HIPAA designated record set.

regulator.agency: NY Attorney General — Data Breach Reporting / SHIELD Act

cited_facts.text: SHIELD Act requires businesses maintaining private information to develop, implement, and maintain reasonable administrative, technical, and physical safeguards for security, confidentiality, and integrity.
SHIELD expands breach definition to unauthorized 'access' (not only acquisition); expands private information to include biometric data, username/email with password, and related credentials.
On breach affecting private information, businesses must notify affected consumers and the NY AG, Department of State, and State Police; AG breach portal submission satisfies multi-agency notice.

citation.source: NY AG — Report a Data Breach

cited_facts.text: PARKED — nysenate.gov GBL §899-aa statute text remains Cloudflare-blocked; requirements sourced from NY AG official SHIELD summary and DFS FAQ cross-reference to §899-aa.

gap.message: SHIELD requirements FOUND via NY AG; primary statute HTML on nysenate.gov remains PARKED.
gap.note: 23 NYCRR Part 500 (DFS) applies to covered financial entities — parallel but not universal medical-practice baseline.
